basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: al-Mulk — “the Sovereignty” — opens with a blessing upon the One who holds the Kingdom in His hand and who created death and life “to test you, to see who among you acts best.” The sūra is a great call to lift the eyes: seven heavens without the least flaw, the gaze that returns “weary, dimmed” from seeking a breach, the birds that “none but the Most Merciful holds up” in flight. Qushayrī opens with a burning basmala: the Name by which hearts are perfumed only by the breeze of His drawing near, by which tears flow only for the burning of His separation or the joy of His union. Onto this he grafts two pearls: on the luminaries of the heaven — God adorned the heaven with stars, and adorned the hearts of His saints with lights: the believers' with faith, the gnostics' with the sun of tawḥīd, the lights of tafrīd, the traces of tajrīd; and on reverent awe (khashya) — it removes all rest: the servant is forever like the grain on the frying-pan, watching for the requital with every breath; the more he grows in obedience, the more he grows in awe. The sūra strings question upon question — who will help you besides the Most Merciful? who will provide for you if He withholds His gifts? who will bring you water if it dries up? — without any of them having an answer.
themes: death and life as a test

versets.ar: تَبَارَكَ الَّذِي بِيَدِهِ الْمُلْكُ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ
versets.tr: tabāraka lladhī bi-yadihi l-mulku wa-huwa ʿalā kulli shayʾin qadīrun
versets.trad: Blessed is He who holds the Kingdom in His hand and who is powerful over all things!
versets.commentaire: tabāraka (تَبارَكَ): blessed be, exalted be. Qushayrī: “He is holy and sublime; from His goodness blessings follow and link without end.” bi-yadihi l-mulk (بِيَدِهِ المُلْك): “by His power, He brings forth what He wills.” The sūra opens not with a command but with praise: to acknowledge that the Kingdom is in His hands is already to lay oneself in His hands. He who holds all needs nothing; and the servant finds his peace in leaving the Kingdom to its King.

mots.racine: ب ر ك
mots.sens: blessed be, exalted be
mots.def: tabāraka: to be a source of blessing, sublime.

mots.racine: ي د ي / م ل ك
mots.sens: He who holds the Kingdom in His hand

mots.racine: ق د ر
mots.sens: He is powerful over all things

versets.ar: الَّذِي خَلَقَ الْمَوْتَ وَالْحَيَاةَ لِيَبْلُوَكُمْ أَيُّكُمْ أَحْسَنُ عَمَلًا وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْغَفُورُ
versets.tr: alladhī khalaqa l-mawta wa-l-ḥayāta li-yabluwakum ayyukum aḥsanu ʿamalan wa-huwa l-ʿazīzu l-ghafūru
versets.trad: He who created death and life to test you, to see who among you acts best, and who is the Mighty, the Forgiving.
versets.commentaire: A capital verse: God created death and life — death named first — for the test. Qushayrī: “He created death and life as a trial of the creatures, to test their gratitude and ingratitude — how they will be, in affliction by patience, and in blessing by gratitude.” The test is not who acts most, but who acts best (aḥsanu ʿamalan): quality, not quantity. And the verse closes on two names of hope: al-ʿAzīz (the Mighty) and al-Ghafūr (the Forgiving) — power tempered with pardon.

mots.racine: خ ل ق / م و ت / ح ي ي
mots.sens: He created death and life

mots.racine: ب ل و
mots.sens: to test you

mots.racine: ح س ن / ع م ل
mots.sens: who among you acts best

mots.racine: ع ز ز / غ ف ر
mots.sens: the Mighty, the Forgiving

versets.ar: الَّذِي خَلَقَ سَبْعَ سَمَاوَاتٍ طِبَاقًا مَّا تَرَىٰ فِي خَلْقِ الرَّحْمَٰنِ مِن تَفَاوُتٍ فَارْجِعِ الْبَصَرَ هَلْ تَرَىٰ مِن فُطُورٍ
versets.tr: alladhī khalaqa sabʿa samāwātin ṭibāqan mā tarā fī khalqi l-raḥmāni min tafāwutin fa-rjiʿi l-baṣara hal tarā min fuṭūrin
versets.trad: He who created seven well-ordered heavens. You see no flaw in the creation of the Most Merciful. Look again! Do you see any breach?
versets.commentaire: mā tarā… min tafāwut (ما تَرى مِنْ تَفاوُت): you see no disparity. Qushayrī: “you see no disproportion that would contradict the traces of wisdom”; and he adds a metaphysical note: “no disparity in His independence from all: the little and the much are equal to Him; the little is no easier for Him nor the much harder, for He transcends ease and toil.” The invitation is to look again (fa-rjiʿi l-baṣar): the perfection of creation yields only to the gaze that lingers, that scrutinizes, that returns. The beauty of the world rewards patient contemplation.

mots.racine: س م و / ط ب ق
mots.sens: seven layered heavens

mots.ar: مَّا تَرَىٰ فِي خَلْقِ الرَّحْمَٰنِ مِن تَفَاوُتٍ
mots.tr: mā tarā fī khalqi l-raḥmāni min tafāwut
mots.racine: ر أ ي / خ ل ق / ف و ت
mots.sens: you see no flaw in the creation of the Most Merciful

mots.racine: ر ج ع / ب ص ر
mots.sens: return the gaze, look again

mots.racine: ف ط ر
mots.sens: do you see any breach

versets.ar: ثُمَّ ارْجِعِ الْبَصَرَ كَرَّتَيْنِ يَنقَلِبْ إِلَيْكَ الْبَصَرُ خَاسِئًا وَهُوَ حَسِيرٌ
versets.tr: thumma rjiʿi l-baṣara karratayni yanqalib ilayka l-baṣaru khāsiʾan wa-huwa ḥasīrun
versets.trad: Then look again, twice over: your gaze will return to you weary, dimmed.
versets.commentaire: yanqalib ilayka l-baṣaru khāsiʾan wa-huwa ḥasīr (يَنْقَلِبْ إلَيْكَ البَصَرُ خاسِئًا وَهُوَ حَسير): the gaze returns to you confounded and exhausted. Qushayrī: “scrutinize closely, repeat the examination and reflection — you will find no defect, no deficiency in His majesty.” The gaze that seeks a flaw returns empty-handed, weary of finding nothing. It is the creature's confession before the Creator's perfection: not that the eye is weak, but that the work is flawless. The exhaustion of the gaze is here a praise — it attests that there is nothing to correct.

mots.racine: ر ج ع / ك ر ر
mots.sens: then look twice again

mots.racine: ق ل ب / خ س أ
mots.sens: the gaze returns to you confounded

mots.racine: ح س ر
mots.sens: and exhausted

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ زَيَّنَّا السَّمَاءَ الدُّنْيَا بِمَصَابِيحَ وَجَعَلْنَاهَا رُجُومًا لِّلشَّيَاطِينِ وَأَعْتَدْنَا لَهُمْ عَذَابَ السَّعِيرِ
versets.tr: wa-laqad zayyannā l-samāʾa l-dunyā bi-maṣābīḥa wa-jaʿalnāhā rujūman li-l-shayāṭīni wa-aʿtadnā lahum ʿadhāba l-saʿīri
versets.trad: We adorned the lowest heaven with lamps and made them missiles against the devils, and We prepared for them the chastisement of the Blaze.
versets.commentaire: A pearl of Qushayrī, in mirror: “He adorned the heaven with stars and constellations, and He adorned the hearts of His saints with all kinds of lights and stars. The hearts of the believers are adorned with faith and attestation, then with verification through the examination of the proof, then with divine assistance toward the quest for meaning. And the hearts of the gnostics (al-ʿārifūn) are adorned with the sun of tawḥīd (Oneness), their spirits with the lights of tafrīd (singling out God alone), their secrets with the traces of tajrīd (stripping away of all else). To each category its lights.” The starry heaven without has its counterpart within: the believer's heart is a firmament God illumines degree by degree.

mots.racine: ز ي ن / ص ب ح
mots.sens: We adorned the lowest heaven with lamps

mots.racine: ج ع ل / ر ج م
mots.sens: and made them missiles against the devils

mots.racine: ع ت د / س ع ر
mots.sens: We prepared for them the chastisement of the Blaze

versets.ar: وَلِلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا بِرَبِّهِمْ عَذَابُ جَهَنَّمَ وَبِئْسَ الْمَصِيرُ
versets.tr: wa-lilladhīna kafarū bi-rabbihim ʿadhābu jahannama wa-biʾsa l-maṣīru
versets.trad: For those who disbelieved in their Lord is the chastisement of Gehenna. What a wretched journey's end!
versets.commentaire: wa-biʾsa l-maṣīr (وَبِئْسَ المَصير): what a wretched destination. To the devils chastised by the meteors (v. 5) is added the fate of the deniers: Gehenna. The word maṣīr (becoming, destination) speaks of the destiny toward which one travels. One does not fall into Gehenna by accident: one becomes it, through a long road of denial. The “wretched journey's end” is the reverse of the return to the Lord: to each the maṣīr his heart has prepared.

mots.racine: ك ف ر / ر ب ب
mots.sens: for those who disbelieved in their Lord

mots.racine: ع ذ ب
mots.sens: the chastisement of Gehenna

mots.racine: ب أ س / ص ي ر
mots.sens: what a wretched journey's end

versets.ar: إِذَا أُلْقُوا فِيهَا سَمِعُوا لَهَا شَهِيقًا وَهِيَ تَفُورُ
versets.tr: idhā ulqū fīhā samiʿū lahā shahīqan wa-hiya tafūru
versets.trad: When they are cast into it, they hear it roar as it boils. It is near
versets.commentaire: shahīqan wa-hiya tafūr (شَهيقًا وَهِيَ تَفور): a roar, as it boils. The Fire is described as a living being: it roars, it boils. The image is terrifying in its nearness — not an inert place but a presence quivering with fury. This personification conveys the intensity of the consequence: what one has fed by denial rises at the end like a beast. The boiling of the Fire echoes the fever of desires one has not stilled.

mots.racine: ل ق ي
mots.sens: when they are cast into it
mots.def: alqā: to cast; passive: to be cast.

mots.racine: س م ع / ش ه ق
mots.sens: they hear it roar
mots.def: shahīq: roar, loud drawing-in of breath.

mots.racine: ف و ر
mots.sens: as it boils

versets.ar: تَكَادُ تَمَيَّزُ مِنَ الْغَيْظِ كُلَّمَا أُلْقِيَ فِيهَا فَوْجٌ سَأَلَهُمْ خَزَنَتُهَا أَلَمْ يَأْتِكُمْ نَذِيرٌ
versets.tr: takādu tamayyazu mina l-ghayẓi kullamā ulqiya fīhā fawjun saʾalahum khazanatuhā a-lam yaʾtikum nadhīrun
versets.trad: to burst with fury. Each time a group is cast into it, its keepers ask them: “Did no warner come to you?”
versets.commentaire: takādu tamayyazu mina l-ghayẓ (تَكادُ تَمَيَّزُ مِنَ الغَيْظ): it nearly bursts with fury. The personified Fire overflows with rage. And the keepers pose the revealing question: a-lam yaʾtikum nadhīr — did no warner come to you? Qushayrī notes the import: none is chastised except after the sending of messengers. The keepers' question is not cruelty but a statement of justice: the sanction presupposes the warning received and rejected. One does not fall for want of being forewarned, but for refusing to hear.

mots.racine: ك و د / م ي ز / غ ي ظ
mots.sens: it nearly bursts with fury
mots.def: tamayyaza: to come apart; ghayẓ: fury.

mots.racine: ل ق ي / ف و ج
mots.sens: each time a group is cast into it

mots.racine: س أ ل / خ ز ن
mots.sens: its keepers ask them

mots.racine: أ ت ي / ن ذ ر
mots.sens: did no warner come to you

versets.ar: قَالُوا بَلَىٰ قَدْ جَاءَنَا نَذِيرٌ فَكَذَّبْنَا وَقُلْنَا مَا نَزَّلَ اللَّهُ مِن شَيْءٍ إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا فِي ضَلَالٍ كَبِيرٍ
versets.tr: qālū balā qad jāʾanā nadhīrun fa-kadhdhabnā wa-qulnā mā nazzala llāhu min shayʾin in antum illā fī ḍalālin kabīrin
versets.trad: They say: “Yes, a warner did come to us, but we cried lies and said: ‘God has sent down nothing; you are only in great error.’”
versets.commentaire: A lucid but too-late confession: balā qad jāʾanā nadhīr fa-kadhdhabnā — yes, a warner came to us, but we denied him. Worse still, they had turned the accusation around: it was the warners they called the misguided. Here is the height of denial: to name the guidance itself an error. On the Day, the truth bursts from their own mouths — but the confession, which would have saved had it preceded death, now serves only to register the loss. To acknowledge too late is to acknowledge in vain.

mots.racine: ق و ل / ج ي أ
mots.sens: they said: yes, a warner came to us

mots.racine: ك ذ ب
mots.sens: but we denied
mots.def: kadhdhaba: to treat as a lie.

mots.racine: ن ز ل
mots.sens: God has sent down nothing

mots.ar: إِنْ أَنتُمْ إِلَّا فِي ضَلَالٍ كَبِيرٍ
mots.tr: in antum illā fī ḍalālin kabīr
mots.racine: ض ل ل / ك ب ر
mots.sens: you are only in great error

versets.ar: وَقَالُوا لَوْ كُنَّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِلُ مَا كُنَّا فِي أَصْحَابِ السَّعِيرِ
versets.tr: wa-qālū law kunnā nasmaʿu aw naʿqilu mā kunnā fī aṣḥābi l-saʿīri
versets.trad: They add: “Had we listened or reasoned, we would not be among the inmates of the Blaze.”
versets.commentaire: law kunnā nasmaʿu aw naʿqil (لَوْ كُنّا نَسْمَعُ أَوْ نَعْقِل): had we listened or reasoned. Qushayrī: “they had no ear of acceptance (samʿ qabūl), so they deserved the chastisement; they listened neither to the counsel of the counsellors, nor to what would have given life to their hearts.” And he sees in it, by contrast, good news for the believers: “they listen and understand what they hear; whoever listens through the Real hears all that is said of the Real, from the mouth of whoever says it” — for his hearing is “from God, by God, in God.” True listening is a spiritual sense, not the mere use of the ear.

mots.racine: س م ع
mots.sens: had we listened

mots.racine: ع ق ل
mots.sens: or reasoned

mots.racine: ص ح ب / س ع ر
mots.sens: we would not be among the inmates of the Blaze
mots.def: aṣḥāb al-saʿīr: the inmates of the blaze.

versets.ar: فَاعْتَرَفُوا بِذَنبِهِمْ فَسُحْقًا لِّأَصْحَابِ السَّعِيرِ
versets.tr: fa-ʿtarafū bi-dhanbihim fa-suḥqan li-aṣḥābi l-saʿīri
versets.trad: They acknowledge their sin. Away, then, with the inmates of the Blaze!
versets.commentaire: fa-ʿtarafū bi-dhanbihim (فَاعْتَرَفوا بِذَنْبِهِمْ): they acknowledge their sin. Qushayrī is decisive: “they acknowledged their fault, but out of the time of acknowledgement (fī ghayri waqti l-iʿtirāf).” That is all: confession has its hour, and that is life. Acknowledged in time, sin opens the door of pardon (cf. the istighfār, s. 71, v. 10); acknowledged too late, it only seals the loss. Suḥqan: away, at a distance — the word of removal. The same acknowledgement that draws near in its time casts away out of its time.

mots.racine: ع ر ف / ذ ن ب
mots.sens: they acknowledged their sin

mots.racine: س ح ق
mots.sens: away with the inmates of the Blaze

versets.ar: إِنَّ الَّذِينَ يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُم بِالْغَيْبِ لَهُم مَّغْفِرَةٌ وَأَجْرٌ كَبِيرٌ
versets.tr: inna lladhīna yakhshawna rabbahum bi-l-ghaybi lahum maghfiratun wa-ajrun kabīrun
versets.trad: Those who fear their Lord in secret will have forgiveness and a great reward.
versets.commentaire: yakhshawna rabbahum bi-l-ghayb (يَخْشَوْنَ رَبَّهُمْ بِالغَيْب): they fear their Lord in secret. A pearl of Qushayrī on khashya: “reverent awe removes all rest: the servant, in his disquiet, is forever like the grain on the frying-pan (al-ḥabb ʿalā l-miqlā), finding calm neither day nor night, watching for the requital with every breath; and the more he grows in obedience to God, the more he grows in awe of God.” Khashya is no servile fear but a loving vigilance, growing with nearness. To fear “in secret” is to revere God where none sees — proof that the awe comes from the heart, not from others' eyes.

mots.racine: خ ش ي / ر ب ب
mots.sens: they fear their Lord

mots.racine: غ ي ب
mots.sens: in secret, the unseen

mots.racine: غ ف ر / أ ج ر
mots.sens: theirs is forgiveness and a great reward

versets.ar: وَأَسِرُّوا قَوْلَكُمْ أَوِ اجْهَرُوا بِهِ إِنَّهُ عَلِيمٌ بِذَاتِ الصُّدُورِ
versets.tr: wa-asirrū qawlakum awi jharū bihi innahu ʿalīmun bi-dhāti l-ṣudūri
versets.trad: Whether you keep your speech secret or proclaim it, He knows perfectly what is in the breasts.
versets.commentaire: ʿalīmun bi-dhāti l-ṣudūr (عَليمٌ بِذاتِ الصُّدور): He knows what the breasts hold. Qushayrī: “He frightened them by His knowledge and invited them to His watch (murāqaba), for He knows the secret and what is more hidden than the secret, and hears the loud voice and the whisper.” Whether the word is hushed or proclaimed, it is known to God. This omniscience is no oppressive surveillance but an invitation to intimacy: since He sees the bottom of the heart, one may as well speak to Him openheartedly. The watch of the heart (murāqaba) is born of this certainty: one is seen, so one lives under the Gaze.

mots.racine: س ر ر / ق و ل
mots.sens: keep your speech secret

mots.racine: ج ه ر
mots.sens: or proclaim it

mots.racine: ع ل م / ص د ر
mots.sens: He knows what the breasts hold

versets.ar: أَلَا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ خَلَقَ وَهُوَ اللَّطِيفُ الْخَبِيرُ
versets.tr: a-lā yaʿlamu man khalaqa wa-huwa l-laṭīfu l-khabīru
versets.trad: Should He not know what He created, He who is the Subtle, the All-Aware?
versets.commentaire: a-lā yaʿlamu man khalaq (أَلا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ خَلَق): should the One who created not know? A luminous argument: the Artisan knows His work better than it knows itself. Qushayrī: “in every part of His creation — beings and traces — are indications of His knowledge and His wisdom.” And two names seal the verse: al-Laṭīf (the Subtle, who penetrates to the finest folds) and al-Khabīr (the All-Aware). God knows the creature from within, down to the depths of the heart. This subtle knowledge is also a gentleness: He knows our weaknesses, and His subtlety (luṭf) is tenderness as much as penetration.

mots.racine: ع ل م / خ ل ق
mots.sens: should He not know what He created

mots.racine: ل ط ف / خ ب ر
mots.sens: the Subtle, the All-Aware
mots.def: laṭīf: subtle, kind; khabīr: perfectly aware.

versets.ar: هُوَ الَّذِي جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الْأَرْضَ ذَلُولًا فَامْشُوا فِي مَنَاكِبِهَا وَكُلُوا مِن رِّزْقِهِ وَإِلَيْهِ النُّشُورُ
versets.tr: huwa lladhī jaʿala lakumu l-arḍa dhalūlan fa-mshū fī manākibihā wa-kulū min rizqihi wa-ilayhi l-nushūru
versets.trad: It is He who made the earth submissive to you; so walk its broad expanses and eat of the provision God grants. To Him is the Resurrection.
versets.commentaire: jaʿala lakumu l-arḍa dhalūlan (جَعَلَ لَكُمُ الأَرْضَ ذَلولًا): He made the earth submissive to you. Qushayrī gives a magnificent inner reading: “if you wish to travel the earth, He has made it easy for you; likewise He has made the soul (al-nafs) submissive: if you require it to harmony, you find it cooperative, following and even outstripping.” The submissive earth is the mirror of the tamed soul: what God does with the ground — making it habitable, walkable — He can do with the rebellious ego, making it docile to the way. Whoever masters his nafs finds in it a mount, not an enemy.

mots.racine: ج ع ل / أ ر ض / ذ ل ل
mots.sens: He made the earth submissive to you

mots.racine: م ش ي / ن ك ب
mots.sens: walk its flanks

mots.racine: أ ك ل / ر ز ق
mots.sens: eat of His provision

mots.racine: ن ش ر
mots.sens: to Him is the Resurrection

versets.ar: أَأَمِنتُم مَّن فِي السَّمَاءِ أَن يَخْسِفَ بِكُمُ الْأَرْضَ فَإِذَا هِيَ تَمُورُ
versets.tr: a-amintum man fī l-samāʾi an yakhsifa bikumu l-arḍa fa-idhā hiya tamūru
versets.trad: Do you feel secure that He who is in heaven will not make the earth swallow you? Then, behold, it quakes...
versets.commentaire: a-amintum (أَأَمِنْتُمْ): do you feel secure? The submissive earth (v. 15) can also swallow. Qushayrī specifies that “He who is in heaven” denotes the angels charged with chastisement. The question shakes man's false assurance: this ground you tread with confidence could engulf you. The precariousness of existence is recalled not to terrify but to awaken gratitude: each moment of stability is a gift, not a due. The one who thinks himself secure by his own means forgets on Whom the very ground beneath his feet depends.

mots.racine: أ م ن / س م و
mots.sens: do you feel secure against Him who is in heaven

mots.racine: خ س ف
mots.sens: that He make the earth swallow you
mots.def: khasafa: to swallow, cause to collapse.

mots.racine: م و ر
mots.sens: and behold, it quakes

versets.ar: أَمْ أَمِنتُم مَّن فِي السَّمَاءِ أَن يُرْسِلَ عَلَيْكُمْ حَاصِبًا فَسَتَعْلَمُونَ كَيْفَ نَذِيرِ
versets.tr: am amintum man fī l-samāʾi an yursila ʿalaykum ḥāṣiban fa-sa-taʿlamūna kayfa nadhīri
versets.trad: Do you feel secure that He who is in heaven will not loose against you a storm of stones? Then you will know the nature of My warning.
versets.commentaire: ḥāṣiban (حاصِبًا): a wind laden with stones, a storm. Qushayrī recalls the fate of the people of Lot, stoned from the sky. And the threat concludes: fa-sa-taʿlamūna kayfa nadhīr — then you will know what My warning is. The warning is no idle word: it has a reality that will be verified. As long as one does not undergo it, one takes it lightly; when it comes to pass, it is too late to gauge its seriousness. Wisdom is to believe the warning before knowing it by ordeal.

mots.racine: ر س ل / ح ص ب
mots.sens: that He loose against you a storm of stones

mots.racine: ع ل م / ن ذ ر
mots.sens: you will know what My warning is

versets.ar: وَلَقَدْ كَذَّبَ الَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ فَكَيْفَ كَانَ نَكِيرِ
versets.tr: wa-laqad kadhdhaba lladhīna min qablihim fa-kayfa kāna nakīri
versets.trad: Those before them had cried lies. How terrible was My reprobation!
versets.commentaire: fa-kayfa kāna nakīr (فَكَيْفَ كانَ نَكير): how great was My reprobation! The argument of history: past peoples denied, and their fate was terrible. Qushayrī: “the profit of their mention is the lesson (iʿtibār) one draws from it, the guarding against what they did, so as not to undergo what they underwent.” The story of the annihilated is not a tale of the past but a mirror of the present: what befell them threatens whoever repeats their refusal. The divine nakīr — the rejection of what rejects Him — is constant across the ages.

mots.racine: ك ذ ب / ق ب ل
mots.sens: those before them denied

mots.racine: ن ك ر
mots.sens: how was My reprobation

versets.ar: أَوَلَمْ يَرَوْا إِلَى الطَّيْرِ فَوْقَهُمْ صَافَّاتٍ وَيَقْبِضْنَ مَا يُمْسِكُهُنَّ إِلَّا الرَّحْمَٰنُ إِنَّهُ بِكُلِّ شَيْءٍ بَصِيرٌ
versets.tr: a-wa-lam yaraw ilā l-ṭayri fawqahum ṣāffātin wa-yaqbiḍna mā yumsikuhunna illā l-raḥmānu innahu bi-kulli shayʾin baṣīrun
versets.trad: Do they not see the birds above them, spreading their wings and folding them? None holds them up but the Most Merciful; truly, He sees all things!
versets.commentaire: mā yumsikuhunna illā l-raḥmān (ما يُمْسِكُهُنَّ إلّا الرَّحْمن): none holds them up but the Most Merciful. An image of infinite delicacy: the bird that spreads and folds its wings in the void is held only by an invisible hand. Qushayrī invites meditation: what holds the bird suspended in the air, if not God? The lesson goes beyond the bird: every being is held at each instant by that unseen hand. As the bird is ignorant of what bears it up, man is often ignorant of the One who holds him in life. To lift the eyes to the birds is to learn to recognize the hand that holds us up.

mots.racine: ر أ ي / ط ي ر
mots.sens: do they not see the birds above them

mots.racine: ص ف ف / ق ب ض
mots.sens: spreading and folding their wings
mots.def: ṣāffāt: spread wings; qabaḍa: to fold.

mots.racine: م س ك / ر ح م
mots.sens: none holds them but the Most Merciful

mots.racine: ب ص ر
mots.sens: He sees all things

versets.ar: أَمَّنْ هَٰذَا الَّذِي هُوَ جُندٌ لَّكُمْ يَنصُرُكُم مِّن دُونِ الرَّحْمَٰنِ إِنِ الْكَافِرُونَ إِلَّا فِي غُرُورٍ
versets.tr: amman hādhā lladhī huwa jundun lakum yanṣurukum min dūni l-raḥmāni ini l-kāfirūna illā fī ghurūrin
versets.trad: Is there anyone who could be an army for you, helping you besides the Most Merciful? The disbelievers are only in illusion.
versets.commentaire: ini l-kāfirūna illā fī ghurūr (إنِ الكافِرونَ إلّا في غُرور): the deniers are only in illusion. Qushayrī poses the decisive question: “if the Most Merciful wills you an ill, who will widen for you what He has narrowed? who will erase what He has fixed? who will advance what He has deferred, or defer what He has advanced?” No army helps against God. Ghurūr is the illusion of believing one has other recourses: alliances, forces, schemes. All of it vanishes before the one Power. To acknowledge that there is no help but Him is to step out of illusion into the real.

mots.ar: أَمَّنْ هَٰذَا الَّذِي هُوَ جُندٌ لَّكُمْ
mots.tr: amman hādhā lladhī huwa jundun lakum
mots.racine: ج ن د
mots.sens: who would be an army for you

mots.racine: ن ص ر
mots.sens: helping you besides the Most Merciful

mots.racine: غ ر ر
mots.sens: the deniers are only in illusion

versets.ar: أَمَّنْ هَٰذَا الَّذِي يَرْزُقُكُمْ إِنْ أَمْسَكَ رِزْقَهُ بَل لَّجُّوا فِي عُتُوٍّ وَنُفُورٍ
versets.tr: amman hādhā lladhī yarzuqukum in amsaka rizqahu bal lajjū fī ʿutuwwin wa-nufūrin
versets.trad: Who is there to provide for you if He withholds His provision? Yet they persist in insolence and aversion.
versets.commentaire: in amsaka rizqah (إنْ أَمْسَكَ رِزْقَهُ): if He withholds His provision. A new question without answer: if God suspends His gifts, who will provide? The provision man believes earned by his labour is but a flow whose source God holds. Let Him withhold it, and none replaces it. But instead of acknowledging this dependence, the deniers persist (lajjū) in arrogance (ʿutuww) and aversion (nufūr). Obstinacy is willful blindness: before the evidence of one's dependence, to refuse to bow. To acknowledge that every gift comes from Him disarms insolence.

mots.racine: ر ز ق
mots.sens: who will provide for you

mots.racine: م س ك / ر ز ق
mots.sens: if He withholds His provision

mots.racine: ل ج ج / ع ت و / ن ف ر
mots.sens: they persist in insolence and aversion

versets.ar: أَفَمَن يَمْشِي مُكِبًّا عَلَىٰ وَجْهِهِ أَهْدَىٰ أَمَّن يَمْشِي سَوِيًّا عَلَىٰ صِرَاطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ
versets.tr: a-fa-man yamshī mukibban ʿalā wajhihi ahdā amman yamshī sawiyyan ʿalā ṣirāṭin mustaqīmin
versets.trad: Is one who walks with face cast down better guided than one who walks upright on a straight path?
versets.commentaire: A striking comparison of two ways of advancing through life: mukibban ʿalā wajhihi (مُكِبًّا عَلى وَجْهِهِ) — bent, face to the ground, stumbling; and sawiyyan ʿalā ṣirāṭin mustaqīm — upright, on a straight path. An image of all existence: one advances bent over his cravings, nose in the dust of the world, tripping at every step; the other walks erect, gaze toward the goal, on the straight road. The question expects no answer: it unveils that the posture of the body reflects that of the soul. To straighten oneself within is already to see where one is going.

mots.racine: م ش ي / ك ب ب / و ج ه
mots.sens: one who walks bent on his face

mots.racine: ه د ي
mots.sens: is he better guided

mots.ar: أَمَّن يَمْشِي سَوِيًّا عَلَىٰ صِرَاطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ
mots.tr: amman yamshī sawiyyan ʿalā ṣirāṭin mustaqīm
mots.racine: س و ي / ص ر ط / ق و م
mots.sens: or one who walks upright on a straight path
mots.def: sawiyy: upright; ṣirāṭ mustaqīm: straight path.

versets.ar: قُلْ هُوَ الَّذِي أَنشَأَكُمْ وَجَعَلَ لَكُمُ السَّمْعَ وَالْأَبْصَارَ وَالْأَفْئِدَةَ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَشْكُرُونَ
versets.tr: qul huwa lladhī anshaʾakum wa-jaʿala lakumu l-samʿa wa-l-abṣāra wa-l-afʾidata qalīlan mā tashkurūna
versets.trad: Say: “It is He who brought you into being and gave you hearing, sight, and hearts. How little you give thanks!”
versets.commentaire: al-samʿa wa-l-abṣāra wa-l-afʾida (السَّمْعَ وَالأَبْصارَ وَالأَفْئِدَة): hearing, sights, hearts. Three gifts for the three doors of knowledge: to hear the truth, to see it, to understand it in the heart. Qushayrī: “He singled you out with hearing, sight, and hearts, and you do not give thanks for the immensity of His gifts.” Qalīlan mā tashkurūn — how little you give thanks! The reproach is gentle but piercing: the very faculties that should lead us to gratitude serve everything but thanking Him. To acknowledge that hearing, sight, the heart are gifts is to begin to use them for the One who gave them.

mots.racine: ن ش أ
mots.sens: say: it is He who brought you into being

mots.racine: س م ع / ب ص ر / ف أ د
mots.sens: and gave you hearing, sights, and hearts
mots.def: samʿ: hearing; abṣār: sights; afʾida: hearts.

mots.racine: ق ل ل / ش ك ر
mots.sens: how little you give thanks

versets.ar: قُلْ هُوَ الَّذِي ذَرَأَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ وَإِلَيْهِ تُحْشَرُونَ
versets.tr: qul huwa lladhī dharaʾakum fī l-arḍi wa-ilayhi tuḥsharūna
versets.trad: Say: “It is He who scattered you over the earth, and to Him you will be gathered.”
versets.commentaire: dharaʾakum fī l-arḍ… wa-ilayhi tuḥsharūn (ذَرَأَكُمْ في الأَرْضِ وَإلَيْهِ تُحْشَرون): He scattered you over the earth and to Him you will be gathered. Two complementary movements: the scattering (dharʾ) that spread you over the earth, and the gathering (ḥashr) that will bring you back to Him. Like seeds the wind disperses then the harvest collects, men are sown then reaped. The verse encloses all existence between two gestures of God: He scattered, He will gather. Between the two, life is but an interval; and to know that the end is the return to Him orients the whole road.

mots.ar: قُلْ هُوَ الَّذِي ذَرَأَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ
mots.tr: qul huwa lladhī dharaʾakum fī l-arḍ
mots.racine: ذ ر أ
mots.sens: say: it is He who scattered you over the earth

mots.racine: ح ش ر
mots.sens: and to Him you will be gathered

versets.ar: وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَٰذَا الْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَادِقِينَ
versets.tr: wa-yaqūlūna matā hādhā l-waʿdu in kuntum ṣādiqīna
versets.trad: They say: “When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?”
versets.commentaire: matā hādhā l-waʿd (مَتى هذا الوَعْد): when this promise? A question of incredulity, as in so many sūras: to demand the date so as to be excused from believing. Qushayrī: God answers in substance “do not hasten the chastisement” and shows how, seeing it come, they will fear and repent. To ask when is to confuse the slowness of the term with its unreality. But what is delayed is not what does not come: the delay is mercy, not denial. Wisdom is not to know the date but to be ready for it.

mots.racine: ق و ل / و ع د
mots.sens: they say: when this promise

mots.racine: ص د ق
mots.sens: if you are truthful

versets.ar: قُلْ إِنَّمَا الْعِلْمُ عِندَ اللَّهِ وَإِنَّمَا أَنَا نَذِيرٌ مُّبِينٌ
versets.tr: qul innamā l-ʿilmu ʿinda llāhi wa-innamā anā nadhīrun mubīnun
versets.trad: Say: “The knowledge is with God alone, and I am only a clear warner.”
versets.commentaire: innamā l-ʿilmu ʿinda llāh (إنَّما العِلْمُ عِنْدَ الله): the knowledge is with God alone. An answer to the challenge (v. 25): knowledge of the Hour belongs to God alone. And the Prophet defines himself with humility: nadhīr mubīn, a mere clear warner. He does not claim to know the when; his mission is not to predict but to forewarn. A beautiful lesson in measure: to acknowledge the bounds of one's knowledge is higher than to feign knowing all. The true servant does not covet the knowledge reserved for God; he carries out the task entrusted to him.

mots.racine: ع ل م
mots.sens: say: the knowledge is with God alone

mots.racine: ن ذ ر / ب ي ن
mots.sens: I am only a clear warner

versets.ar: فَلَمَّا رَأَوْهُ زُلْفَةً سِيئَتْ وُجُوهُ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَقِيلَ هَٰذَا الَّذِي كُنتُم بِهِ تَدَّعُونَ
versets.tr: fa-lammā raʾawhu zulfatan sīʾat wujūhu lladhīna kafarū wa-qīla hādhā lladhī kuntum bihi taddaʿūna
versets.trad: When the disbelievers see it drawn near, their faces will be horror-struck. They will be told: “This is what you were calling for!”
versets.commentaire: raʾawhu zulfa (رَأَوْهُ زُلْفَة): they will see it drawn near. What they demanded in defiance (v. 25, “when then?”) is at last shown them — and their faces are stricken. Sīʾat wujūhuhum: their faces darken with dread. The irony is cutting: hādhā lladhī kuntum bihi taddaʿūn — this is what you were calling for. One ends by obtaining what one demanded with arrogance, and then measures its horror. The nearness of the chastisement dispels at a stroke the illusion (the ghurūr of v. 20): what one took from afar for an idle threat imposes itself up close as a crushing reality.

mots.racine: ر أ ي / ز ل ف
mots.sens: when they see it drawn near

mots.racine: س و أ / و ج ه
mots.sens: the faces of the disbelievers darken

mots.racine: د ع و
mots.sens: this is what you were calling for

versets.ar: قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَهْلَكَنِيَ اللَّهُ وَمَن مَّعِيَ أَوْ رَحِمَنَا فَمَن يُجِيرُ الْكَافِرِينَ مِنْ عَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ
versets.tr: qul a-raʾaytum in ahlakaniya llāhu wa-man maʿiya aw raḥimanā fa-man yujīru l-kāfirīna min ʿadhābin alīmin
versets.trad: Say: “Have you considered? Whether God destroys me and those with me, or shows us mercy, who will protect the disbelievers from a painful chastisement?”
versets.commentaire: A subtle argument: whether I die or God shows me mercy, what does it change for you? fa-man yujīru l-kāfirīn — who will protect the deniers? The adversaries awaited the Prophet's death as their deliverance. But his disappearance would not save them: their problem is not the messenger, it is their own denial. One does not free oneself by removing the one who disturbs; one frees oneself only by changing one's heart. The Prophet's fate, dead or alive, alters nothing of their exposure to chastisement: only their return to God could.

mots.racine: ر أ ي
mots.sens: have you considered
mots.def: araʾaytum: what do you think of.

mots.racine: ه ل ك
mots.sens: if God destroys me and those with me

mots.racine: ر ح م
mots.sens: or shows us mercy

mots.racine: ج و ر
mots.sens: who will protect the deniers

versets.ar: قُلْ هُوَ الرَّحْمَٰنُ آمَنَّا بِهِ وَعَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْنَا فَسَتَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ هُوَ فِي ضَلَالٍ مُّبِينٍ
versets.tr: qul huwa l-raḥmānu āmannā bihi wa-ʿalayhi tawakkalnā fa-sa-taʿlamūna man huwa fī ḍalālin mubīnin
versets.trad: Say: “He is the Most Merciful! We believe in Him and in Him we place our trust! You will soon know who is in manifest error.”
versets.commentaire: āmannā bihi wa-ʿalayhi tawakkalnā (آمَنّا بِهِ وَعَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلْنا): we believe in Him and entrust all to Him. Qushayrī: “to Him we have committed all our affairs in their entirety (jumlatan fawwaḍnā).” Two linked movements: the faith (īmān) of the heart and the trusting surrender (tawakkul) of one's whole existence. To believe is not enough; one must also lay the weight of one's affairs in His hands. Tawakkul is the fruit of faith: since He alone helps (v. 20), provides (v. 21), and holds the bird in the air (v. 19), to Him alone one surrenders. And the future will decide: you will know who is in manifest error.

mots.racine: ر ح م
mots.sens: say: He is the Most Merciful

mots.racine: أ م ن / و ك ل
mots.sens: we believe in Him and entrust all to Him
mots.def: āmana: to believe; tawakkala: to entrust oneself.

mots.ar: فَسَتَعْلَمُونَ مَنْ هُوَ فِي ضَلَالٍ مُّبِينٍ
mots.tr: fa-sa-taʿlamūna man huwa fī ḍalālin mubīn
mots.racine: ع ل م / ض ل ل
mots.sens: you will know who is in manifest error

versets.ar: قُلْ أَرَأَيْتُمْ إِنْ أَصْبَحَ مَاؤُكُمْ غَوْرًا فَمَن يَأْتِيكُم بِمَاءٍ مَّعِينٍ
versets.tr: qul a-raʾaytum in aṣbaḥa māʾukum ghawran fa-man yaʾtīkum bi-māʾin maʿīnin
versets.trad: Say: “Have you considered? If your water were swallowed up by the earth, who then would bring you flowing water?”
versets.commentaire: A closing verse, and a final question without answer: in aṣbaḥa māʾukum ghawran (إنْ أَصْبَحَ ماؤُكُمْ غَوْرًا) — if your water sank away, beyond reach. Fa-man yaʾtīkum bi-māʾin maʿīn: who would bring you flowing water? Qushayrī: “all these questions are arguments against them, and none had an answer.” Water, the very source of life, does not depend on man: let it withdraw into the depths, and no human force makes it rise again. The sūra of Sovereignty thus closes on the confession of absolute dependence: everything — the heaven, the provision, the bird, the water — is held by the hand of the One who holds the Kingdom. To acknowledge this dependence is no humiliation but truth: it is to render the Kingdom to its King.

mots.racine: ر أ ي
mots.sens: have you considered
mots.def: araʾaytum: what do you think of.

mots.racine: ص ب ح / غ و ر
mots.sens: if your water sank away, dried up
mots.def: ghawr: water sunk, out of reach.

mots.racine: أ ت ي / م ع ن
mots.sens: who would bring you flowing water
